description: Reducing immunogenicity of gene therapies and biologics - vector immunity, protein engineering, and immune modulation.

facts.context: Pre-existing neutralizing antibodies (NAbs) against AAV vectors affect therapy by:
facts.truth: preventing vector transduction, requiring NAb screening before dosing
distractors: enhancing vector delivery to target cells

facts.context: Engineered AAV capsids can evade immunity by:
facts.truth: modifying surface epitopes while preserving transduction (directed evolution)
distractors: using identical capsids at higher doses

facts.context: PEGylation of lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) for mRNA delivery:
facts.truth: extends circulation time but can induce anti-PEG antibodies affecting repeat dosing
replacing murine framework regions with human sequences while preserving CDRs

facts.context: T-cell epitope deimmunization of therapeutic proteins involves:
facts.truth: identifying and mutating MHC-II binding peptides to reduce CD4+ T cell responses

facts.context: Immune tolerance to gene therapy transgenes can be induced by:
facts.truth: hepatocyte-specific expression which promotes regulatory T cell induction

facts.context: Transient immunosuppression during AAV gene therapy:
facts.truth: can prevent capsid-directed T cell responses that eliminate transduced cells

facts.context: CpG depletion in therapeutic DNA/mRNA sequences reduces:
facts.truth: TLR9-mediated innate immune activation and inflammatory responses

facts.context: Modified nucleosides (pseudouridine, m1Ψ) in therapeutic mRNA:
facts.truth: reduce innate immune sensing by TLR7/8 and RIG-I while maintaining translation

facts.context: Complement activation by therapeutic nanoparticles can cause:
facts.truth: CARPA (complement activation-related pseudoallergy) requiring slow infusion
